metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or specify) how case-type context (operator-attested vs. codebase-verifiable) is threaded into the pointwise llm-judge call for `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded`, so the carve-out in the rubric text is actually actionable by the judge — e.g., by embedding the case type in the finding's `criterion` or `detail` field, or by switching to a non-pointwise rubric that receives the full case output.
findings.finding: The AC5 case-conditional rubric mechanism assumes the pointwise judge has access to case-level metadata (specifically, whether the case is operator-attested), but the existing eval harness runs llm-judge scorers pointwise over individual findings — the judge receives a finding and the static rubric text, not the case's ticket_context or its [operator-attested] tag. Without threading case-type context into the judge call, the 'source citation counts as valid grounding for operator-attested cases' carve-out in the rubric text is a no-op: the judge cannot apply it because it cannot determine which case type produced the finding.
findings.location: AC5 — src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml, finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded rubric; src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py (pointwise judge path)

findings.finding: The eval solver (`eval_solver.py`) has no mechanism to seed ticket COMMENTS into disposable eval fixtures — it only writes `ticket_context` as the ticket description. The three new operator-attested eval cases (CV-operator-attested-concrete, CV-operator-attested-vague, CV-attestation-with-injection) require attestation evidence that the verifier reads via `show_ticket`; the plan describes this as 'a ticket_context comment naming a change id + vote outcome + timestamp,' but the solver calls `rebar.create_ticket(description=ticket_context)` and never calls `rebar.comment()` on the disposable ticket. If the implementer embeds the attestation inside the description (the only available vehicle), the test works but validates description-embedded attestations — not the comment-as-evidence path that production operators will use. If the implementer tries to seed a real comment, the solver needs extension that is out of scope and unspecified.
findings.location: AC4 (four new eval dataset cases) / `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.py` lines 193–201 / `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `comments: list[str]` field to eval case data and extend `eval_solver.py`'s `completion-verifier` arm: after `create_ticket`, iterate `case.get('comments') or []` and call `rebar.comment(tid, body, repo_root=root)` for each. This faithfully seeds the production evidence channel and makes the fixture self-documenting. If description-embedding is intentional instead, note it explicitly in the eval spec comment and rename the AC4 description from 'a ticket_context comment' to 'ticket_context text that includes an attestation'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the AC1 proof command by supplying the target filename on every `grep` invocation (e.g., repeat `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md` after each pattern, or collect all patterns into one `grep -qiE 'operator-attested|codebase-verifiable|verifiable specific|command|attestation'` call).
findings.finding: AC1's proof command uses `…` (Unicode ellipsis) as a placeholder for the target filename in each intermediate `grep` invocation; only the final `grep` has the explicit filename. An executing agent running this command literally would have the intermediate greps read from stdin (blocking) or error on a non-existent file named `…`, causing the proof to fail even when the file is correct.
findings.suggested_fix:
grep -qiE 'operator-attested' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md && grep -qiE 'codebase-verifiable' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md && grep -qiE 'verifiable specific' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md && grep -qiE '\bcommand\b' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md && grep -qiE '\battestation\b' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md

Or equivalently as a single call:
grep -qiP '(?=.*operator-attested)(?=.*codebase-verifiable)' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md is not right — use separate greps each with the filename, or: grep -c '' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md > /dev/null && grep -qi 'operator-attested' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md && grep -qi 'codebase-verifiable' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md && grep -qi 'verifiable specific' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md && grep -qi 'attestation' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `grep -qi 'remediation' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md` to AC1's or AC3's proof commands to confirm the prompt actually instructs the verifier to populate the per-finding remediation field.
findings.finding: The remediation instruction in `completion_verifier.md` is a named deliverable in Scope and What-item-4 ('the prompt instructs the verifier to populate it for an unmet operator-attested criterion') but neither AC1's grep commands nor AC3's proof commands verify that the prompt file actually contains this instruction. AC1 greps for 'operator-attested', 'codebase-verifiable', 'verifiable specific', 'command', and 'attestation' — not 'remediation'. AC3's proof is a unit test on the Python field + grep in contracts.py + make test. An implementer could write the contracts.py field and satisfy all AC proofs while omitting the prompt instruction entirely.
findings.location: Scope IN (completion_verifier.md bullet), What item 4, AC1 proof greps, AC3 proof
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1 or AC3 proof, append: `&& grep -qi 'remediation' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md` — or add an explicit sentence to AC3: 'Proof also includes: the verifier prompt contains an instruction to populate `remediation` for unmet operator-attested findings, confirmed by grep.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC5's proof to specifically grep within the rubric section of the eval YAML for the case-conditional text (e.g. `grep -A5 'finding_cites_criterion' ...eval.yaml | grep -qi 'operator-attested'`), or reference a specific rubric field/key that would only exist if the rubric was actually updated.
findings.finding: AC5's proof grep (`grep -qi 'operator-attested' src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml`) is trivially satisfied by the four new dataset case names/labels alone and does not specifically verify that the `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` rubric text contains the case-conditional carve-out. The rubric change is the entire substance of AC5, but the proof cannot distinguish 'rubric updated' from 'rubric unchanged, case names contain the string'.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC5 proof grep with one that targets the rubric definition specifically, such as: `grep -A 10 'finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded' src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml | grep -qi 'operator-attested'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC6's proof obligation (or add an AC4 scorer/assertion) to verify that the `CV-operator-attested-vague` (and/or `CV-unmarked-fallback-codebase`) case produces a finding with a non-null `remediation` field, so the per-finding remediation behavior is behaviorally tested, not just schema-tested.
findings.finding: The only test that `remediation` is populated for an unmet operator-attested criterion is AC3's Pydantic round-trip unit test, which verifies the field EXISTS and serializes — not that the verifier prompt actually emits a non-null value. AC6's proof statement requires recording 'case ids + PASS/FAIL + gate result' but does not require observing a non-null `remediation` in the output. Since the field is optional (`str | None`), a verifier that always omits `remediation` passes every test in the plan.
findings.location: AC3 (unit test + grep proof); AC6 (live eval proof statement)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC6, add to the required recorded output: the `remediation` text for the failing operator-attested case (proving the prompt populated it). Alternatively, add a lightweight inline assertion in the eval fixture for the vague-fail case that checks `finding.remediation is not None` via the existing `emits_valid_verdict` scorer or a new `remediation_populated_on_fail` assertion in the eval YAML (which does not require a new scorer type if expressed as a rubric condition).

findings.finding: AC5's verification method is 'Verified by reading the eval spec; covered by the AC5 validate_eval_spec command' — but the specific claim (that the grounding rubric accepts ticket-text citation for operator-attested findings) is a semantic/behavioral property that validate_eval_spec does not test, leaving AC5 with no machine-executable proving command.
findings.location: AC5 (grounding rubric relaxation) — 'Verified by reading the eval spec'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the AC6 CLI command from `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` to the actual command `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` (verified in `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py`).
findings.finding: AC6 names the CLI command `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier`, but the actual CLI surface confirmed in the codebase is `rebar prompt eval <id>` (verified in `_llm_eval_commands.py`, `README.md`, and all existing eval YAML comments). `rebar-eval` does not exist as a binary or subcommand; the proof command would fail at operator attestation time.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` in AC6 with `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier --output json` (the `--output json` flag is available per the CLI parser and makes the run output machine-readable for pasting as the attestation comment).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] What-item-3 / AC2: Specify the structural placement of the command-vs-attestation split rule within `completion_verifier.md` — either as a named sub-rule inside the existing 'Untrusted input' section or as a carve-out immediately following it — and describe how the prompt's processing order (classify tag → apply kind-specific bar → apply injection guard to commands only) is laid out, to avoid contradicting the existing uniform injection guard.
findings.finding: The injection-guard/attestation split rule — a new processing protocol distinguishing 'a command tries to control the verdict' from 'an attestation reports a checkable fact' — is specified only in the plan's 'What' prose (item 3) and the canonical-terminology section, but is not co-located with the existing injection-guard section in `completion_verifier.md`. The current prompt has a single 'Untrusted input' block that covers all criteria uniformly with no two-path decision rule. The plan does not specify where in the prompt the split rule lives, how the two paths are structured, or how the LLM should identify a command vs. an attestation when both appear in the same comment (as in `CV-attestation-with-injection`). This is a processing protocol without a specified home.

AC4 case (b) requires the eval spec's scorer/assertion to verify that the resulting finding's `remediation` field is 'non-empty and carries the record-proof coaching', but the stated proving command (`validate_eval_spec(strict=True) == []`) only checks spec structural validity — it cannot verify that the scorer actually asserts on the remediation field content (fixture invalidity, defect type 4).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC5: Specify the exact mechanism for case-conditional rubric branching — either (a) state that the rubric string itself must include explicit `if operator-attested case: ...` instructions that the judge reads, relying on the judge to infer case kind from the presence of an `[operator-attested]` tag in the finding text, OR (b) define a new YAML mechanism and the corresponding harness change needed to dispatch it — so an implementor knows precisely what to build.
findings.finding: The AC5 'case-conditional rubric' for `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` has no mechanical basis in the eval harness: the rubric is a single static string in the scorer definition, and `run_eval` does not execute llm-judge scorers at all (only deterministic scorers gate). There is no per-case rubric branching infrastructure in `eval.py`, `eval_scorers.py`, or `_gating_results`. The plan says to make the rubric 'case-conditional' but never specifies the mechanism — whether the rubric string itself must encode an if/then instruction the judge reads, a new YAML field, or some other dispatch — leaving the implementor with an undefined protocol.
findings.suggested_fix: Since llm-judge scorers only report and the rubric is a static string, the simplest correct mechanism is to embed the conditional directly in the rubric text: instruct the judge to detect whether the finding's criterion text contains `[operator-attested]` and apply the appropriate grounding bar. Document this in AC5 explicitly.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC4(b) / What-item-3: Explicitly require the completion-verifier prompt (and the `CV-operator-attested-vague` fixture's expected behavior description) to emit a `source`-kind citation referencing the ticket-text evidence (or its absence) for every operator-attested FAIL finding, so `emits_valid_verdict`'s citation-presence check is satisfied.
findings.finding: The new `CV-operator-attested-vague` eval case (expect: fail, no `files` fixture) will produce a FAIL verdict whose findings have no file citations — only possible `source`-kind ticket-text citations. The existing `emits_valid_verdict` scorer (`_schema_verdict`) requires every FAIL finding to carry ≥1 citation of any kind (`if not (f.get('citations') or [])`). The plan does not explicitly instruct the completion-verifier prompt to always emit a `source` citation for operator-attested FAIL findings (citing the missing specifics), nor does it address this scorer interaction. If the verifier emits a finding with only `detail` and no `citations`, `emits_valid_verdict` will fail `CV-operator-attested-vague`, breaking the behavioral proof.
findings.location: AC4(b) `CV-operator-attested-vague`; src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py `_schema_verdict` lines 168-170; plan What-item-4 (per-finding remediation instruction)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the prompt's operator-attested verdict section: 'For an unmet operator-attested criterion, cite the specific attestation text you evaluated (or note its absence) as a `source` citation.' Also add a note in AC4(b) that the expected finding must carry a source citation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the updated `completion_verifier.md` prompt explicitly instructs the verifier to emit a `source` citation (citing the ticket-text attestation) on every FAIL finding for an operator-attested criterion, so new eval cases satisfy the existing `emits_valid_verdict` gating scorer's citation requirement.
findings.finding: The `emits_valid_verdict` scorer (_schema_verdict) is an unenumerated consumer whose citation requirement will affect all four new eval cases: it checks `if not (f.get('citations') or []): return ScoreResult(True, False, 'a FAIL finding lacks a source citation')`. The two FAIL cases (CV-operator-attested-vague, CV-unmarked-fallback-codebase) must emit findings with at least one citation, or they will fail the gating scorer for a reason unrelated to the operator-attested logic being tested. The plan's Scope and AC4 do not enumerate _schema_verdict as a consumer constraint that the new prompt changes must satisfy.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the operator-attested FAIL path in completion_verifier.md: 'For an operator-attested criterion that is NOT MET, emit a `source` citation describing which attestation was found (or that none was found) — a bare FAIL finding with no citation will be rejected by the verdict contract.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cleanup step (or an explicit inventory/grep) identifying every file that uses prior synonyms for the two criterion kinds, and include updating or cross-referencing those files in the Scope (IN) list to avoid stranded stale references.
findings.finding: The plan declares 'all prior synonyms retired' for the old terminology (synonyms for 'operator-attested' and 'codebase-verifiable') but provides no inventory of where those synonyms currently appear in existing docs, prompts, ADRs, or runbooks — and no cleanup step to update or deprecate them. This means existing documents could silently retain stale terms after the change lands.
findings.location: Canonical terminology section ('all prior synonyms retired') and Scope (IN/OUT)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line grep step to the scope (e.g., `grep -r 'operational-criterion\|runtime-criterion\|<prior synonym>' docs/ src/`) and list any hits as IN-scope for a terminology note or cross-reference to ADR 0043.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan how `completion_verdict.schema.json`'s `findings.items` entry will be updated to document the per-finding `remediation` field (e.g., replace the bare `$ref` with an `allOf: [{$ref: ...}, {properties: {remediation: ...}}]` wrapper, or add a top-level `description` to the items object noting the additional property).
findings.finding: The `completion_verdict.schema.json` items entry for `findings` currently references `common.schema.json#/$defs/finding` with no inline schema augmentation. The plan says to 'document the per-finding `remediation` in the `findings` items description' — but the items entry is a bare `$ref` with no sibling `description` property. Documenting `remediation` requires changing this items entry from `{ "$ref": "common.schema.json#/$defs/finding" }` to an inline schema (e.g., adding an `allOf` wrapper or a sibling description), which is an edit to `completion_verdict.schema.json` the plan does not describe. The plan's scope for this file says only 'document the per-finding `remediation` in the `findings` items description' without specifying the mechanical change needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fifth eval case `CV-malformed-tag-fallback` (e.g., `[operator_attested]` with underscore) whose only evidence is a concrete attestation comment; `expect: fail` (malformed tag → codebase-verifiable → comment alone ≠ MET), exercising the near-miss→default fallback path described in What §5.
findings.finding: No eval case tests the explicitly-described malformed/near-miss tag fallback. The plan names `[operator_attested]` (underscore) as a canonical near-miss that must fall back to codebase-verifiable, yet all four AC4 cases cover only the (a) well-formed tag + concrete, (b) well-formed tag + vague, (c) well-formed tag + injection, and (d) completely untagged scenarios. The malformed-tag path is therefore untested, meaning a verifier bug that promotes `[operator_attested]` to operator-attested status would go undetected.
findings.location: AC4 / AC5 — completion-verifier.eval.yaml dataset cases; also AC-authoring convention section (What §5)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `completion-verifier.eval.yaml`: a case with AC text `- [ ] [operator_attested] …` (underscore variant), a ticket_context attestation that would satisfy the operator-attested bar if the tag were recognized, repo carrying no matching code; set `expect: fail`. This proves the fallback fires for near-miss tags.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the per-finding `remediation` field on `VerdictFinding` (AC3) will be surfaced to the author distinctly from the already-present top-level `COMPLETION_REMEDIATION_GUIDANCE` (e.g., in CLI output or the transition-close path), or reconsider whether criterion-specific guidance is better expressed as part of the `detail` field the verifier already populates per-finding.
findings.finding: The plan adds `remediation: str | None` to `VerdictFinding` (the per-finding inner model in `contracts.py`) alongside the already-existing top-level `COMPLETION_REMEDIATION_GUIDANCE` string that `reconcile_verdict` already attaches to every FAIL verdict. The top-level guidance already instructs authors to 'add a comment to this ticket that documents the evidence'. The per-finding field would provide criterion-specific guidance but has only ONE identified producer (the LLM for operator-attested unmet criteria) and ONE consumer cited in the plan (the AC3 unit test round-trip). No production read-path is named in the Scope or ACs that would surface the per-finding `remediation` to the author differently from the top-level field.

AC4's `CV-operator-attested-vague` case requires the assertion 'finding's `remediation` field is non-empty and carries the record-proof coaching', but no deterministic scorer in the `REGISTRY` checks per-finding `remediation` content, and the plan does not name a new scorer to add. The `test_new_eval_specs.py` auto-coverage check (`validate_eval_spec(strict=True)`) only validates scorer names are registered — it cannot enforce this per-finding field assertion unless a new scorer is registered.

AC4 specifies that the `CV-operator-attested-vague` case must assert 'the resulting finding's `remediation` field [is] non-empty and carries the record-proof coaching,' but the eval framework's gating mechanism works only through registered deterministic scorer names (`known_scorer_names()` in `eval_scorers.py`) or non-gating LLM judges. No registered scorer checks per-finding `remediation`. If the implementer adds a new deterministic scorer name for this assertion without registering it in `REGISTRY`, `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` rejects the spec. If expressed as part of the LLM judge rubric, the assertion is advisory-only (non-gating). The plan specifies neither a new scorer name nor which of the two mechanisms carries this assertion.

The AC4b eval case (CV-operator-attested-vague) requires an assertion that the resulting finding's `remediation` field is non-empty, but no scorer — deterministic or LLM-judge — is defined or named in the plan to enforce this assertion. The REGISTRY in eval_scorers.py has no entry that inspects a finding-level `remediation` field, and the existing `emits_valid_verdict` scorer only checks `citations` presence (line 168-170), not `remediation`. An executor building the eval case has no specified mechanism to satisfy the assertion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a deterministic scorer `operator_attested_finding_has_remediation` to eval_scorers.py that, for cases tagged with `criterion_kind: operator-attested` (add a case-level metadata key), inspects each FAIL finding for a non-empty `remediation` field. Register it in REGISTRY and name it in the `CV-operator-attested-vague` case's scorer list.

findings.finding: The AC4c eval case (CV-attestation-with-injection) requires 'a scorer/rubric asserting the PASS was reached by crediting the factual specific while ignoring the command,' but the plan never specifies which scorer name, whether it is a new deterministic scorer or an LLM-judge, or how it is wired. No matching scorer name exists in eval_scorers.py REGISTRY. The `expect: pass` + `no_false_fail_on_complete` scorer pair would fire only on PASS/FAIL verdict — it cannot distinguish *why* a PASS was reached (factual credit vs. command obeying).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either cite prior empirical evidence that inline-tag mode-switching is reliably recognized by LLM evaluators, or replace 'deterministic from the tag' with 'rule-based with a conservative default (absent/malformed tag → stricter codebase bar)' and note that any misclassification fails toward the stricter bar rather than the permissive one.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the classification is deterministic from the tag' for an LLM evaluator — a strong capability claim for a probabilistic system — but cites no prior art, no empirical baseline, and no ablation to support that a one-token inline tag reliably switches evaluation mode across model updates or prompt variations. This is an unverified capability assertion under T1(c).
findings.location: Rationale section: "the classification is deterministic from the tag, so behavior on all existing (untagged) tickets is unchanged"
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the classification is deterministic from the tag' with: 'The classification rule is tag-driven with a fail-safe default: absent or malformed tag → codebase-verifiable (the stricter bar). Any LLM misclassification of the tag therefore degrades toward the stricter bar, not the permissive one — the backward-compatibility risk is bounded.' Then add an ADR-0043 note confirming this was an explicit design choice, not an assumption of perfect tag recognition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art summary to the plan body (not just to the forthcoming ADR) noting what indirect-prompt-injection mitigations were considered and why the command/attestation split within a single prompt was chosen over alternatives such as a separate evidence field, cryptographic attestation, or out-of-band submission.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel 'command vs. attestation' discriminator as a prompt-injection defence within a single LLM prompt — a pattern not found elsewhere in the codebase — but the plan itself contains no prior-art discussion of how similar systems draw this boundary. The ADR-0043 is listed as a deliverable (AC7) that will document 'indirect-prompt-injection prior art and why haste-not-deception makes the command/attestation split acceptable', but the plan does not demonstrate that this prior art was consulted before committing to the design.
findings.location: What item 3 (injection guard split); AC7 (ADR-0043 requirement); Rationale section (threat model framing)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
